items.question: Make it passive (person first): 'They gave me a medal.'
options: I was given a medal.
A medal was given me.
I was gave a medal.
Me was given a medal.
explanations: Correct! Person as subject: 'I was given a medal'.
This needs 'to me': 'A medal was given to me'.
'Was gave' uses the wrong form; use 'given'.

items.question: Make it passive (thing first): 'They sent her a parcel.'
options: A parcel was sent to her.
A parcel was sent her.
A parcel was send to her.
Her was sent a parcel.
explanations: Correct! Thing as subject needs 'to': 'A parcel was sent to her'.
'Was send' uses the wrong form; use 'sent'.

items.question: 'The company offered him a job.' → (natural passive)
options: He was offered a job.
A job was offered him.
He was offer a job.
Him was offered a job.
explanations: Correct! Person first is more natural: 'He was offered a job'.

items.question: 'Someone has told us the news.' → (person first)
options: We have been told the news.
The news has been told us.
We have been telled the news.
Us have been told the news.
explanations: Correct! Present perfect passive, person first: 'We have been told the news'.
'Telled' isn't a word; the participle is 'told'.

items.question: Add the missing word: 'A letter was sent ___ every customer.'
options: to
for
at
by
explanations: Correct! Thing as subject takes 'to': 'sent to every customer'.
'By' would mark the doer, not the receiver.

items.question: 'They will show the visitors the garden.' → (person first, future)
options: The visitors will be shown the garden.
The garden will be shown the visitors.
The visitors will be show the garden.
The visitors are shown the garden tomorrow.
explanations: Correct! Future passive, person first: 'The visitors will be shown the garden'.
'Will be show' uses the wrong form.

items.question: Correct this: 'I was given me a second chance.'
options: I was given a second chance.
I was given to me a second chance.
Me was given a second chance.
The sentence is already correct.
explanations: Correct! Don't repeat the object: 'I was given a second chance'.
'Given to me' repeats the subject as an object.

items.question: Correct this: 'A prize was given the winner.'
options: A prize was given to the winner.
A prize was given for the winner.
A prize was gave to the winner.
The winner was given to a prize.
explanations: Correct! Thing as subject needs 'to': 'given to the winner'.
'Given to a prize' reverses the meaning.

items.question: Which passive is usually more natural in English?
options: The one starting with the person (She was sent an email).
The one starting with the thing (An email was sent to her).
They are equally natural in every case.
Neither; two-object verbs have no passive.
explanations: Correct! English tends to put the person first: 'She was sent an email'.
The thing-first version is possible but less common.

items.question: Which verb does NOT normally take two objects?
options: arrive
give
send
offer
explanations: Correct! 'Arrive' has no object at all, so no two-object passive.
